text:
When first discovering this almost a decade ago, I understood hardly any of the references and was still having an absolute blast. The songs got stuck in my head after several rewatches and I keep humming them even to the present day. This then got me thinking of how many phenomenal games, music, series', even books me and so many other people discovered and enjoyed *exclusively * because some kid on his dad's laptop decided to edit it alongside some genie from an obscure shitty game saying "Squadula!", and share it with others.
Pre-2010s Youtube was such a miracle of nature, before a "meta" for content was figured out, and the mentality for many users was as pure as "here's a thing I like, with my spin on it", with no desire to generate likes, money, fame, sponsorships or anything of that sort. As much as I would love that today, any corner of the internet can hope to offer creations so unique, bizarre and innocent as this era of Youtube, I don't think its possible.

Some of these might want a touch up, if I may criticize?

The most glaring issue is that you'd have a hard time keeping in pace with a metronome. During some of the notes where you have to put a lot in one spot, you end up extending it. May be a fault in the program used, but I can't say for sure.
And secondly, attention to chords was a major issue in the AVGN theme. You would quite likely benifit a lot from an extra Morshu, or one that didn't hum the same note over and over again, since it gives off chords that aren't really in the song.

That is the end of my critisism.

text:
The good old days of Youtube, as we go into the new year having suffered massive blows from all-around. The COPPA crisis looms overhead as rumors of video and channel purges spread like wildfire. Videos get falsely claimed and revenue redirected into the hands of those who steal. No defense is even correctly considered for those affected. One laments about the current state, wishing nothing more than to go back and re-live the simple joys of what used to be.


One can only pray that someone out there can save the memory of the old days of YouTube, and someone can save this site from completely going past the extinction point. Unfortunately, all too often it feels like there is no hope that YouTube will recover from these blows. At least YouTube is letting us reminisce about the past seemingly before dissolving into internet history.




Aight, rant about how shitty the future of the site looks like over.
